supabase_cases.case_summary:
According to the Complaint, Exxon Mobil Corporation explores for and produces crude oil and natural gas in the U.S. and abroad. One of the Company’s most important oil and gas properties is in the Permian Basin, which is currently the highest-producing oil field in the U.S. In 2017, the Company acquired acreage in the Permian Basin, in an area called the Delaware Basin, for $6.6 billion.
The Complaint alleges that throughout the Class Period, Defendants made materially false and misleading statements, and failed to disclose material adverse facts about the Company's business, operational, and compliance policies. Specifically, the Complaint alleges Defendants made false and/or misleading statements and failed to disclose to investors that: (i) Exxon forced its employees to use unrealistic assumptions regarding the timelines for well drilling in the Permian Basin; (ii) the foregoing assumptions served to artificially inflate the value of the Company's well operations in the Permian Basin; (iii) the foregoing conduct, when revealed, subjected Exxon to a heightened risk of regulatory investigation and oversight; and (iv) as a result, the Company's public statements were materially false and misleading at all relevant times.
The Court issued an Order consolidating cases and appointing Lead Plaintiffs and Counsel on June 10, 2021. All future docketing was ordered to be done in the lead case 21-CV-00194.
Lead Plaintiffs filed an amended Complaint on September 10, 2021. Defendants filed a Motion to Dismiss the amended Complaint on November 24. On September 29, 2022, the Court issued an Order granting Defendants' Motion to Dismiss. Plaintiffs were given leave to amend the Complaint.
On October 31, 2022, Lead Plaintiffs filed a second amended Complaint. Defendants filed a Motion to Dismiss the second amended Complaint on November 14. On August 24, 2023, the Court issued an Order granting in part and denying in part Defendants' Motion to Dismiss.

According to the law firm press release, Exxon Mobil Corporation ("Exxon" or the Company) is a multinational oil and gas company and the world’s largest publicly traded company.
The Complaint alleges that throughout the Class Period, Exxon repeatedly highlighted the strength of its business model and its transparency and reporting integrity, particularly with regard to its oil and gas reserves and the value of those reserves. Exxon’s public statements were materially false and misleading when made as they failed to disclose: (a) that Exxon’s own internally generated reports concerning climate change recognized the environmental risks caused by global warming and climate change; (b) that, given the risks associated with global warming and climate change, the Company would not be able to extract the existing hydrocarbon reserves Exxon claimed to have and, therefore, a material portion of Exxon’s reserves were stranded and should have been written down; and (c) that Exxon had employed an inaccurate “price of carbon” – the cost of regulations such as a carbon tax or a cap-and-trade system to push down emissions – in evaluating the value of certain of its future oil and gas prospects in order to keep the value of its reserves materially overstated. As a result of Defendants’ positive Class Period statements, Exxon common stock traded at artificially inflated prices, reaching a Class Period high of more than $95 per share, and the rating agencies maintained Exxon’s AAA debt rating – the highest – permitting Exxon to sell $12 billion of corporate debt at extraordinarily favorable rates during the Class Period.
Through a series of partial disclosures issued by different news sources between mid-August 2016 and late September 2016, the market learned that federal regulators were actively scrutinizing Exxon’s reserve accounting related to climate change and global warming and its refusal to write down any of its oil and gas reserves in the face of declining global oil prices. On these disclosures, the price of Exxon common stock fell as low as $82.54 per share on September 20, 2016, down more than 13% from the stock’s Class Period high, erasing billions of dollars of market capitalization.
Then on October 28, 2016, before the open of trading, Exxon issued a release announcing its financial results for the quarter ended September 30, 2016. Exxon disclosed that it might be forced to write down nearly 20% of its oil and gas assets. Specifically, the Company acknowledged that it might have to write down 3.6 billion barrels of oil sand reserves and one billion barrels of other North American reserves that Exxon now conceded were not profitable to produce under current prices. In response to this news, the price of Exxon common stock fell more than $2 per share on October 28, 2016, on unusually high trading volume.
On May 3, 2017, the Court appointed Lead Plaintiffs and Counsel. Lead Plaintiff filed an amended Complaint on July 26, 2017. On September 26, Defendants filed a Motion to Dismiss the amended Complaint. On August 14, 2018, the Court issued an Order granting in part and denying in part Defendants' Motion to Dismiss.
Lead Plaintiffs filed a Motion for Class Certification on December 21, 2018. On August 21, 2023, the Court issued an Order granting in part and denying in part the Class Certification Motion.

Exxon Mobil Corporation ("Exxon" or the Company) is a multinational oil and gas company and the world’s largest publicly traded company.
The Complaint alleges that Exxon and its CEO violated Sections 14(a) and 14 (e) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934. Specifically, the Complaint alleges that during the Class Period, the Defendants issued materially false and misleading financial statements contained in a proxy filed with the Securities and Exchange Commission that, inter alia, overstated the Company 's financial condition by inflating revenue in violation of General Accepted Accounting Principles. In particular, Plaintiff alleges that the proxy and accompanying materials failed to account for impaired assets. Plaintiff seeks to recover on behalf of all those who purchased or otherwise held Mobil stock and exchanged it for Exxon stock.
On July 20, 2004, the Court issued an Order appointing Lead Plaintiff and Counsel. Lead Plaintiff filed an amended Complaint on October 4.
Defendants filed a Motion to Dismiss the Complaint on March 18, 2005. On September 14, the Court issued an Order granting Defendants' Motion to Dismiss.
Lead Plaintiff filed a notice appealing the Court's Dismissal Order on October 12, 2005. The Court of Appeals affirmed the District Court's ruling on October 30, 2007.

risk_card.data_coverage_note: SCA database contains ~6,982 filings (~35% of estimated universe). NERA/Cornerstone are authoritative full-universe references.

screening_questions.question: Has the company disclosed any material weakness or significant deficiency in internal controls in the last 3 years?
screening_questions.why_it_matters: Material weaknesses are the #1 precursor to restatements. Restatements shift avg settlement from $48M to $152M — a 3.2x multiplier.
screening_questions.upgrade_criteria: Clean SOX 404 opinions for 3+ consecutive years with no significant deficiencies
screening_questions.downgrade_criteria: Any material weakness disclosed, especially if unremediated. Significant deficiency in revenue recognition or financial close process.

screening_questions.question: Is the company subject to active or recent regulatory investigation by SEC, DOJ, state AG, or sector-specific regulator (FDA, CFPB, EPA)?
screening_questions.why_it_matters: SEC/DOJ investigations are the strongest severity amplifier in the entire database. When a regulatory action SCA also involves a restatement, avg settlement is $980M.
screening_questions.upgrade_criteria: No regulatory investigations. Clean regulatory history. Routine examinations only.
screening_questions.downgrade_criteria: Active investigation by any government body. CID or subpoena disclosed. Wells notice received.

screening_questions.question: Does the company have complex revenue recognition (ASC 606)? Multiple performance obligations, variable consideration, or long-term contracts?
screening_questions.why_it_matters: Revenue recognition complexity is the single most common allegation type in accounting fraud SCAs. ASC 606 implementation created new attack surfaces.
screening_questions.upgrade_criteria: Simple revenue model (product ships, revenue recognized). Single performance obligation per contract.
screening_questions.downgrade_criteria: Multiple element arrangements, bill-and-hold, percentage of completion, channel stuffing risk, significant variable consideration

screening_questions.question: Is this a repeat SCA target? How many prior filings, and what were the outcomes?
screening_questions.why_it_matters: 25% of all SCAs involve companies sued before. 46 companies have been sued 4+ times. Prior history is a base-rate multiplier — plaintiffs bar has institutional memory.
screening_questions.upgrade_criteria: No prior SCAs. Or: 1 prior SCA that was dismissed, >5 years ago.
screening_questions.downgrade_criteria: Any prior settled SCA. 2+ prior filings regardless of outcome. Active ongoing SCA.

screening_questions.question: Does the company operate in a heavily regulated industry with binary approval/denial risk (FDA, CFPB, EPA, banking regulators)?
screening_questions.why_it_matters: Binary regulatory outcomes (approved/denied) create the sharpest stock drops and cleanest plaintiff narratives.
screening_questions.upgrade_criteria: Diversified regulatory exposure across multiple products/jurisdictions. No single regulatory decision is existential.
screening_questions.downgrade_criteria: Single-product company dependent on one regulatory approval. Banking company under consent order. Pharma with single PDUFA date driving valuation.

screening_questions.question: Has the stock experienced a >15% single-day drop in the last 12 months?
screening_questions.why_it_matters: Stock drops >15% are the mechanical trigger for plaintiffs firms to evaluate filing. Larger drops = larger damages = higher settlement.
screening_questions.upgrade_criteria: No single-day drop >10% in 12 months. Low volatility stock.
screening_questions.downgrade_criteria: Any single-day drop >15%. Multiple drops >10%. Stock down >30% from 12-month high.

screening_questions.question: What is the delta between GAAP and non-GAAP earnings? Is the non-GAAP adjustment >20% of GAAP?
screening_questions.why_it_matters: Large GAAP/non-GAAP deltas create the "earnings quality" allegations that dominate accounting fraud SCAs. Plaintiffs argue the non-GAAP figures are misleading.
screening_questions.upgrade_criteria: Non-GAAP adjustments <10% of GAAP, limited to standard items (stock comp, amortization)
screening_questions.downgrade_criteria: Non-GAAP adjustments >20% of GAAP, includes unusual addbacks, company leads investor communications with non-GAAP

screening_questions.question: CFO tenure: how long has the current CFO been in role? Any CFO changes in last 18 months?
screening_questions.why_it_matters: The MSA lesson: a 4-month CFO was missed in diligence. Short CFO tenure correlates with both (a) inherited accounting problems and (b) new CFO discovering and disclosing issues.
screening_questions.upgrade_criteria: CFO tenure >3 years, no recent changes, prior CFO departed for normal reasons
screening_questions.downgrade_criteria: CFO tenure <18 months, especially if predecessor departed abruptly or "to pursue other opportunities"

screening_questions.question: What is short interest as a % of float? Has it increased >50% in the last 90 days?
screening_questions.data_source: FINRA short interest data, short seller report databases
screening_questions.why_it_matters: Rising short interest is a leading indicator of both stock drops and short-seller reports that trigger SCAs (Hercules Capital/HTGC pattern).
screening_questions.upgrade_criteria: Short interest <3% of float, stable or declining.
screening_questions.downgrade_criteria: Short interest >8% of float, or any increase >50% in 90 days. Named in short seller report.

screening_questions.question: What is the market cap? How does it map to the settlement severity curve for the relevant scenario?
screening_questions.why_it_matters: Market cap is the strongest predictor of settlement size — its the damages denominator. A $50B company with a 20% drop has a $10B damages pool.
screening_questions.upgrade_criteria: Market cap <$1B with simple business model — severity likely <$10M even if case settles.
screening_questions.downgrade_criteria: Market cap >$10B — enters the zone where P90 settlements exceed $100M. Tower adequacy becomes critical.

screening_questions.question: Is the auditor a Big 4 firm? Any auditor changes in the last 24 months?
screening_questions.why_it_matters: Non-Big 4 auditors correlate with higher restatement risk. Auditor changes mid-cycle are a red flag for disagreements on accounting treatment.
screening_questions.upgrade_criteria: Big 4 auditor, same firm for 5+ years, clean opinions
screening_questions.downgrade_criteria: Non-Big 4 auditor, or auditor change within 24 months, especially if prior auditor resigned (vs. was replaced)

search_terms_used: "EXXON MOBIL CORP" securities class action settlement amount
